To address this research gap, we propose a technique that starts with the early identification of validation concerns from a stakeholder perspective, which we use to systematically design tests that drive a scenario-based modeling and analysis of system requirements.
We evaluate our methodology by applying it at KOSTAL (Tier1 supplier) and within student projects as part of the masters program Embedded Systems Engineering.
We demonstrate that the MBSE methodology is suitable to process real-world stakeholder requirements.
Consequently, we ensure that the resulting test specification is suitable to validate the identified stakeholder requirements.
To address this, we propose an ontology that integrates the concepts presented in ref. [18] for a comprehensive and continuous validation system modeling.
The validation activities in turn rely on test specifications as a result of the test design phase.
Consequently, system requirements and test specifications determine the subsequent system design and validation, and hence the overall system quality.

This study introduces the Shared Nearest Neighbor Enhanced District Heating Anomaly Detection (SHEDAD) approach, designed to approximate the DH network topology and allow for local anomaly detection without disclosing sensitive information, such as substation locations.
